text: The surgical team uses vessel loops to encircle and isolate the identified tumor-specific segmental arteries and a perforating upper pole renal artery within the renal hilum, preparing for super selective clamping.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic bulldog clamps to initially clamp the tumor-specific tertiary and quaternary segmental arteries, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the tumor excision by dissecting the overlying Gerota's fascia and freeing the kidney from its attachments for better mobilization. During the tumorectomy, bleeding occurs, prompting the surgeon to adjust the clamping from the quaternary to the tertiary branch to improve exposure conditions.
The surgeon clamps the perforating upper pole renal artery at the posterior aspect of the kidney, which was previously identified during preoperative planning.

The surgeon progresses with the tumor excision by detaching the capsule of the renal mass from the normal kidney parenchyma, revealing a cleavage plane. Once the tumor is completely excised, the deep tumor bed reconstruction begins using a III-O V-lock suture.

The surgeon removes three laparoscopic bulldog clamps from the kidney, utilizing an early unclamping technique after securing the first suture. The warm ischemia time is noted to be thirty-three minutes.

The surgeon completes the tumor bed reconstruction using additional III-O V-lock sutures to achieve hemostasis of the renal parenchyma. Due to the residual bleeding from the large tumor bed, a Tacosil hemostatic patch is applied to control the bleeding.
